skill.name: Frontend Guardrails
skill.description: Professional frontend standards for building, scaffolding, extending, or reviewing any UI or frontend project. Framework-agnostic guidance for React, Vue, Angular, Svelte, and plain JavaScript.
skill.summary: Standing policy set that keeps AI-generated frontend code consistent, reusable, secure, and production-quality by default.
skill.author: ieze-king

security_audit.summary: This skill is pure markdown documentation containing frontend engineering policy and best-practice guidance. The static analyzer flagged 192 potential issues, but on evaluation all are false positives: backticks in markdown inline code (e.g., `useState`, `localStorage`) were matched as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution', and security guidance that explicitly warns AGAINST using `localStorage` for tokens was flagged as 'Browser storage access'. The skill contains no executable code, no network calls, no file system access, and no obfuscation. It is safe to publish.

low_findings.title: Static analyzer false positives: backticks and storage mentions in markdown
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 154 instances of 'external_commands' (backticks), 33 'weak cryptographic algorithm' patterns, and several 'browser storage access' patterns. All of these match on markdown inline-code syntax and security-warning text within the documentation files (security.md, react.md, vue.md, angular.md, svelte.md, accessibility.md, notifications.md, ecosystem.md). The skill contains no executable code, no shell scripts, and no Ruby. The 'browser storage access' flags (e.g., references/security.md:18, references/react.md:35) refer to the security guidance that tells developers to PREFER httpOnly cookies OVER `localStorage`/`sessionStorage` for tokens. The 'system reconnaissance' flags are matches on the word 'system' in phrases like 'design system' and 'build system'. No actual reconnaissance, command execution, or unsafe crypto usage exists.

content.user_title: Ship frontend code that's consistent, secure, and production-ready by default
content.value_statement: AI-generated frontend code drifts into duplication, inconsistent styling, and security mistakes without explicit standards. This skill supplies a portable baseline of 16 universal principles plus framework-specific guidance, so professional output is the default and requires no extra prompting.
actual_capabilities: Enforces 16 universal frontend principles covering reusability, design tokens, responsiveness, state management, security, and accessibility.
Provides framework-specific implementation guidance for React/Next.js, Vue/Nuxt, Angular, and Svelte/SvelteKit.
Applies a self-audit checklist before declaring any task complete, covering duplication, security, polish, and scope discipline.
Warns against common security mistakes like putting secrets in `localStorage`, shipping unsanitized HTML, and trusting client-side route guards.
Recommends composing with mature linters, formatters, and design tools rather than re-implementing them inline.
Scales rigor to project size: applies fundamentals everywhere, but does not impose enterprise tooling on small or beginner projects.
limitations: Out of scope by design: backend, CI/CD, databases, deployment, error monitoring, and visual-regression testing are not covered.
Not a replacement for mature tooling: it routes to ESLint, Lighthouse, Context7, and a11y linters rather than duplicating their rules.
Provides principles and patterns, not a runnable library: it has no executable code to install.
Yields to existing project conventions and explicit user instructions; it is a baseline, not a mandate.

use_cases.title: Scaffolding a new React/Next.js project
use_cases.description: Use when starting a new frontend project to get consistent structure, design tokens, security defaults, and a self-audit checklist applied from the first commit.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers starting a new project

use_cases.title: Reviewing AI-generated UI code
use_cases.description: Use as a review pass over generated components to catch duplication, hardcoded values, unsanitized HTML, secrets in client code, and missing accessibility.

use_cases.title: Teaching AI assistants your team's standards
use_cases.description: Use as a standing skill so every AI session follows the same accessibility, security, state-management, and naming conventions without re-prompting.

prompt_templates.title: Apply standards to a new component
prompt_templates.prompt: I am adding a new [component name] to my [React/Vue/Angular/Svelte] project. Read the matching reference file and apply the universal principles: reuse existing components where possible, use design tokens (no hardcoded values), keep state at the right level, handle loading and empty states, label inputs, ensure keyboard operability, and avoid `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`/`v-html`/`{@html}` with untrusted content. After implementing, run the self-audit checklist and report which standards shaped the result.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a generated file for guardrail violations
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the file at [path] against the frontend guardrails. Flag any of: duplicated markup or logic, hardcoded colors/fonts/spacing, missing loading or empty states, secrets or API keys in client code, unsanitized HTML, missing alt text, missing labels, missing keyboard handlers, and unused or speculative code. For each issue, cite the line and explain the fix in one sentence.

prompt_templates.title: Add auth or token handling safely
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to add [login flow / session handling / token refresh] to my [framework] app. Read references/security.md and the framework-specific security section first. Use httpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies for tokens, never `localStorage`. Keep all secret-bearing logic server-side. Re-check authorization on the backend on every request. Show the resulting code with comments explaining each security decision.

prompt_templates.title: Compose with the existing ecosystem
prompt_templates.prompt: Before writing custom code, identify what tooling the project already has. Check `package.json` and config files for ESLint, Prettier, a11y plugins, and design-token systems. Recommend the smallest set of additions (e.g., `eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y`, Lighthouse, Context7 MCP) instead of re-implementing their rules. Do not introduce CI, TypeScript, or test suites into a project that does not already use them.

output_examples.input: Generate a login form for a React app using tokens.
output: Form is built from existing Input and Button components, not raw inputs.
Tokens are stored in httpOnly cookies set by the backend, not in `localStorage` or React state.
Each field has an associated `<label>`; errors appear inline next to the field via `aria-describedby`.
Submit button shows a loading state and is disabled while the request is in flight.
Failed login shows the error message inline; success navigates to the original destination.
All API calls go through a single `apiClient` module; no `fetch` calls scattered in components.

output_examples.input: Add a notification system for save, error, and session-expired events.
output: All notifications route through one shared `useNotification()` hook and a single Toast component.
Save success uses a non-urgent toast with `role="status"`; session-expired uses a persistent banner with `role="alert"`.
Toasts auto-dismiss after a few seconds for success/info; errors stay until dismissed.
Color and icon are paired; meaning is never carried by color alone.
Placement is consistent across the app (top-right) and uses design tokens, not raw values.
No native `alert()` or `confirm()` calls anywhere in the app.

output_examples.input: Review this file for guardrail violations.
output: Line 12: hardcoded hex `#3b82f6` — replace with the `color.primary` token from the theme.
Line 24: state `userData` is duplicated from props — derive it during render instead.
Line 38: raw `fetch` call in a presentational component — move to the `apiClient` module.
Line 51: `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` on untrusted content — sanitize with DOMPurify first or remove.
Line 67: `<input>` has no associated `<label>` — add one and link via `htmlFor`/`id`.
Line 80: token stored in `localStorage` — move to an httpOnly cookie set by the backend.

best_practices: Detect the existing stack first by reading `package.json` and config files; never impose a stack the project does not use.
Reuse or extend existing components, hooks, and tokens before building something new; duplication is the most common source of repeated bugs.
Apply the self-audit checklist before declaring any task done, and remove debug artifacts, stubs, mock data, and dead code.
anti_patterns: Do not put secrets, API keys, or tokens in any client-exposed env var (anything prefixed `NEXT_PUBLIC_`, `VITE_`, `PUBLIC_`, or in `environment.ts`/`runtimeConfig.public`).
Do not use `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` (React), `v-html` (Vue), `{@html}` (Svelte), `bypassSecurityTrustHtml` (Angular), or `innerHTML` on untrusted content.
Do not rely on client-side route guards, hidden buttons, or disabled fields for security; the backend must re-check authorization on every request.

faq.question: Does this skill work with my framework?
faq.answer: Yes. It ships framework-specific guidance for React/Next.js, Vue/Nuxt, Angular, and Svelte/SvelteKit. For any other framework, apply the 16 universal principles directly.

faq.question: Does it run any code or install any tools?
faq.answer: No. It is a markdown policy set that guides the AI's behavior. It does not execute code, make network calls, or read files. It may recommend tools to install in your project, but only with explicit human approval.

faq.question: Will it override my existing project conventions?
faq.answer: No. The user's explicit instructions and the project's existing conventions always win. The skill provides a baseline; it yields to your choices and only flags genuine risks like secrets in client code.

faq.question: Is it safe to use on auth, payments, or user data work?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill contains a dedicated security reference that enforces httpOnly cookies, no secrets in client code, server-side authorization checks, and HTTPS. Use the security-specific prompt template when starting sensitive work.

faq.question: Does it cover backend, CI/CD, or deployment?
faq.answer: No. These are intentionally out of scope. The skill stays client-side and composes with separate tools (Lighthouse, CodeRabbit, Socket, Snyk) for those areas.

faq.question: How does it avoid over-engineering small projects?
faq.answer: It scales rigor to project size. The fundamentals (DRY, design tokens, responsiveness, security basics, accessibility) always apply, but heavyweight infrastructure like CI pipelines, TypeScript, or test suites is only introduced when the project already uses it or the user asks.
